name: Bog Lake

context:
absence of larger gas bubbles because of the following reasons: (a) after the fall turnover, most CH$_4$ stored in surface sediments was released while small bubbles that remained as “free stationary gas” were unable to break the pressure threshold to be released (Casper et al., 2000; DelSontro, McGinnis, Wehrli, & Ostrovsky, 2015; Flury, Glud, Premke, & Mcginnis, 2015; Martinez & Anderson, 2013); (b) temperature close to the sediments dropped to 4°C in early winter (December 2014), and hence, surficial CH$_4$ production was strongly restricted and may have prevented CH$_4$ bubble formation (DelSontro et al., 2016); (c) low temperature increased CH$_4$ solubility, thereby limiting CH$_4$ bubble formation; and (d) surficial sediment methanogenesis was inhibited by the presence of O$_2$. However, we acknowledge that our ebullition evidence is a gross observation, as the pathway has been reported in several lakes in cold months (Walter, Zimov, Chanton, Verbyla, & Chapin, 2006; Wik, Crill, Bastviken, Danielsson, & Norbäck, 2011). In our study, we exclusively sampled on 1 day each month, which is not sufficient to accurately estimate this emission pathway. In the future, higher temporal resolution of sampling (by diurnal sampling, sampling several days per month and spatial analysis) is required to reduce uncertainties in CH$_4$ ebullition from aquatic ecosystems, as previously recommended by Wik, Thornton, et al. (2016). However, other methods used in the literature contain large uncertainties; DelSontro et al. (2011) indicated that the acoustic technology has large coefficients of variation that exceed 100%, while the classic randomised bubble trap method is highly biased and underestimates results (Walter Anthony & Anthony, 2013). Newer gas analysers and the combination with established techniques may help to determine CH$_4$ diffusion and ebullition in freshwater ecosystems. Zhao et al. (2015) suggested such a method as a tool for assessing diffusion and ebullition, while Grasset et al. (2016) was one of the first studies to include ebullition data from these newer gas analysers.

In the context of CO$_2$, Lake Grosse Fuchskuhle can be classified as an acidic lake, in which CO$_2$ is the major inorganic carbon species (Cole & Prairie, 2009) and where the availability of allochthonous and autochthonous inorganic carbon is in some ecosystems a very important driver of CO$_2$ emissions to the atmosphere (Tank et al., 2018; Tranvik et al., 2009; Weyhenmeyer et al., 2015). During turnover events, with the exception of spring in the SW basin, peaks of CO$_2$ fluxes were detected jointly with high CO$_2$ stored in the water column. The surplus CO$_2$ for both basins likely did not completely originate from surficial (0–20 cm, data not shown) sediments (1.0 ± 0.5 g C/m$^2$, mean ± $SD_y$, pooled basins average), as stored quantities were one order of magnitude lower than the mass of CO$_2$ emitted (13.0 ± 4.6 g C/m$^2$, mean ± $SD_y$, pooled basins average) and dissolved in the water column (9.3 ± 4.3 g C/m$^2$, mean ± $SD_y$, pooled basins average). This suggests inputs of CO$_2$ directly from external sources of inorganic carbon and indirectly from mineralisation of internal or external DOC (Brothers et al., 2014; Engel et al., 2018; Maberly et al., 2013; Tank et al., 2018; Tranvik et al., 2009; Weyhenmeyer et al., 2015). The reduction in DOC in the NE basin potentially accounted for the 41% reduction in total annual CO$_2$ emissions. Moreover, CH$_4$ emissions decreased to a similar extent (53%), but cannot, as previously discussed, be adequately explained by the reduced availability of DOC to the pelagic community. Ducharme-Riel, Vachon, Del Giorgio, and Prairie (2015) remarked on the complexity of lake dynamics since interaction between physical and biological factors regulates CO$_2$ dynamics. Sources of CO$_2$ and contributions by different sources need to be elucidated (Drake et al., 2018; Engel et al., 2018; Tank et al., 2018).

It is important to note that we could not observe any CO$_2$ ebullition with the new gas analyser used in this study, but we acknowledge that our device was not sensitive enough to confirm CO$_2$ in bubbles for several reasons: (a) CO$_2$ is highly soluble in water, and therefore, bubbles trapped in sediments might have had low CO$_2$ concentration; (b) the remaining CO$_2$ concentration in gas bubbles could have been re-dissolved through the water column before bubbles were emitted to the atmosphere (McGinnis et al., 2011); (c) there was a high CO$_2$ concentration inside the chamber (atmospheric concentration, ca. 380 ppm) at the initial time of sampling (to observe CO$_2$ bubbles it would be necessary to exceed this threshold). Similarly, the analyses of Casper et al. (2000) and Sepulveda-Jauregui et al. (2015) determined that only 1% of CO$_2$ was emitted by ebullition and 99% by diffusion in small shallow lakes.

Together, our results may help to identify the environmental factors that regulate carbon emissions in small lakes. In freshwater systems, temperature controls the biological activity across food webs, the length of stratification periods and gas solubility. High temperatures amplified CH$_4$ emissions (Sepulveda-Jauregui et al., 2018), which led to markedly higher amounts of CH$_4$ stored and emitted from the basins in the hotter summer of 2014 with a clear correlation between CH$_4$ emissions and temperature. Similar responses to regional temperature have been shown in ponds, in small shallow, and medium-sized lakes (Bartosiewicz, Laurion, & Macintyre, 2015; Bartosiewicz et al., 2016; Encinas Fernández et al., 2014; Liikanen, Huttunen, Valli, & Martikainen, 2002; Miettinen et al., 2015; Natchimuthu et al., 2014; Room et al., 2014) and in enclosure experiments (Audet et al., 2017; Yvon-Durocher et al., 2017), suggesting that in predicted warming scenarios, an increase in carbon greenhouse gas emissions can be expected in lake ecosystems regardless of DOC inputs and their biogeochemical effects in the water column (e.g., in stratification, temperature regimes, pH, microbial metabolism and CO$_2$ dynamics).

Despite the long-term division of Lake Grosse Fuchskuhle that created two very different stable ecosystems with regard to carbon supply, the CH$_4$ and to some extent CO$_2$, emissions to the atmosphere were mainly determined by DOC-independent environmental conditions, as suggested by direct correlations between gas flux and temperature and between pH and dissolved gases. However, pH is an indirect factor associated with DOC correlation with dissolved gas concentrations. Therefore, the correlation between pH and dissolved CH$_4$ and CO$_2$ in the SW basin implied that DOC can play an important role in greenhouse gas emissions (Drake et al., 2018). In the case of CH$_4$, previous studies on methanogenesis in sediments of this acidic bog lake showed that the pathways to form CH$_4$ were similar to those in pH-neutral lakes (Conrad et al., 2010). name: acidic bog lake

context:
absence of larger gas bubbles because of the following reasons: (a) after the fall turnover, most CH$_4$ stored in surface sediments was released while small bubbles that remained as “free stationary gas” were unable to break the pressure threshold to be released (Casper et al., 2000; DelSontro, McGinnis, Wehrli, & Ostrovsky, 2015; Flury, Glud, Premke, & Mcginnis, 2015; Martinez & Anderson, 2013); (b) temperature close to the sediments dropped to 4°C in early winter (December 2014), and hence, surficial CH$_4$ production was strongly restricted and may have prevented CH$_4$ bubble formation (DelSontro et al., 2016); (c) low temperature increased CH$_4$ solubility, thereby limiting CH$_4$ bubble formation; and (d) surficial sediment methanogenesis was inhibited by the presence of O$_2$. However, we acknowledge that our ebullition evidence is a gross observation, as the pathway has been reported in several lakes in cold months (Walter, Zimov, Chanton, Verbyla, & Chapin, 2006; Wik, Crill, Bastviken, Danielsson, & Norbäck, 2011). In our study, we exclusively sampled on 1 day each month, which is not sufficient to accurately estimate this emission pathway. In the future, higher temporal resolution of sampling (by diurnal sampling, sampling several days per month and spatial analysis) is required to reduce uncertainties in CH$_4$ ebullition from aquatic ecosystems, as previously recommended by Wik, Thornton, et al. (2016). However, other methods used in the literature contain large uncertainties; DelSontro et al. (2011) indicated that the acoustic technology has large coefficients of variation that exceed 100%, while the classic randomised bubble trap method is highly biased and underestimates results (Walter Anthony & Anthony, 2013). Newer gas analysers and the combination with established techniques may help to determine CH$_4$ diffusion and ebullition in freshwater ecosystems. Zhao et al. (2015) suggested such a method as a tool for assessing diffusion and ebullition, while Grasset et al. (2016) was one of the first studies to include ebullition data from these newer gas analysers.

In the context of CO$_2$, Lake Grosse Fuchskuhle can be classified as an acidic lake, in which CO$_2$ is the major inorganic carbon species (Cole & Prairie, 2009) and where the availability of allochthonous and autochthonous inorganic carbon is in some ecosystems a very important driver of CO$_2$ emissions to the atmosphere (Tank et al., 2018; Tranvik et al., 2009; Weyhenmeyer et al., 2015). During turnover events, with the exception of spring in the SW basin, peaks of CO$_2$ fluxes were detected jointly with high CO$_2$ stored in the water column. The surplus CO$_2$ for both basins likely did not completely originate from surficial (0–20 cm, data not shown) sediments (1.0 ± 0.5 g C/m$^2$, mean ± $SD_y$, pooled basins average), as stored quantities were one order of magnitude lower than the mass of CO$_2$ emitted (13.0 ± 4.6 g C/m$^2$, mean ± $SD_y$, pooled basins average) and dissolved in the water column (9.3 ± 4.3 g C/m$^2$, mean ± $SD_y$, pooled basins average). This suggests inputs of CO$_2$ directly from external sources of inorganic carbon and indirectly from mineralisation of internal or external DOC (Brothers et al., 2014; Engel et al., 2018; Maberly et al., 2013; Tank et al., 2018; Tranvik et al., 2009; Weyhenmeyer et al., 2015). The reduction in DOC in the NE basin potentially accounted for the 41% reduction in total annual CO$_2$ emissions. Moreover, CH$_4$ emissions decreased to a similar extent (53%), but cannot, as previously discussed, be adequately explained by the reduced availability of DOC to the pelagic community. Ducharme-Riel, Vachon, Del Giorgio, and Prairie (2015) remarked on the complexity of lake dynamics since interaction between physical and biological factors regulates CO$_2$ dynamics. Sources of CO$_2$ and contributions by different sources need to be elucidated (Drake et al., 2018; Engel et al., 2018; Tank et al., 2018).

It is important to note that we could not observe any CO$_2$ ebullition with the new gas analyser used in this study, but we acknowledge that our device was not sensitive enough to confirm CO$_2$ in bubbles for several reasons: (a) CO$_2$ is highly soluble in water, and therefore, bubbles trapped in sediments might have had low CO$_2$ concentration; (b) the remaining CO$_2$ concentration in gas bubbles could have been re-dissolved through the water column before bubbles were emitted to the atmosphere (McGinnis et al., 2011); (c) there was a high CO$_2$ concentration inside the chamber (atmospheric concentration, ca. 380 ppm) at the initial time of sampling (to observe CO$_2$ bubbles it would be necessary to exceed this threshold). Similarly, the analyses of Casper et al. (2000) and Sepulveda-Jauregui et al. (2015) determined that only 1% of CO$_2$ was emitted by ebullition and 99% by diffusion in small shallow lakes.

name: Reservoir

context:
water, completely dry or experience wet and dry cycles of different time periods. In north-eastern Germany, kettle holes are widely distributed and hence important landscape components. With a density of up to 4,000 per \( \mathrm{km}^2 \) they are mainly situated in agricultural landscapes (Kalettka et al. 2001; Lischeid and Kalettka 2012).

Kettle hole types have been classified according to different hydro-geomorphic characteristics reflecting their high heterogeneity (Kalettka and Rudat 2006). They differ widely with respect to groundwater inflow, surface runoff, internal carbon (C) assimilation and mineralization, macrophyte abundance, and land-use of the respective catchments. As a result, kettle holes fulfill important hydrological and ecological functions affecting the water balance of the surrounding landscape (Hayashi et al. 2003; Gerke et al. 2010). Generally, they are very sensitive to changing hydrological conditions and undergo pronounced wet-dry cycles.

Today, small aquatic systems such as kettle holes are widely recognized as hotspots of C turnover in the landscape (Waiser and Robarts 2004; Cole et al. 2007; Tranvik et al. 2009; Aufdenkampe et al. 2011; Premke et al. 2016). Initial studies of C cycling in small ponds or impoundments show that these systems accumulate high biomasses and can constitute a major and currently underestimated C sink (Cole et al. 1988; Downing et al. 2008). Other studies, however, show that kettle holes also can be significant sources of important greenhouse gases such as \( \mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O} \), \( \mathrm{CH}_4 \) and \( \mathrm{CO}_2 \), which is further exacerbated by eutrophication (Merbach et al. 2002). One study conducted in two wetlands in the prairie region of the North American Great Plains, points towards an important role of bacteria in the metabolism of these systems (Waiser and Robarts 2004). Yet, most of the research on microbial C cycling has been conducted in small, shallow lakes and artificial, small standing inland waters with much less known about the microbiology and biogeochemistry of kettle holes.

Kettle holes generally contain high concentrations of C and nutrients such as nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) (e.g. Lischeid and Kalettka 2012). On the one hand, high N and P concentrations can facilitate high primary production rates (Schindler 1978), and nutrient additions in combination with food web manipulations have been shown to turn net-heterotrophic lakes into net-autotrophic systems (Cole et al. 2000). On the other hand, it is also known that the net metabolic balance in lakes varies continuously along a gradient of dissolved organic carbon (DOC) concentration: as DOC increases, primary production may decrease and lake metabolism becomes progressively more heterotrophic (Tranvik 1988; Prairie et al. 2002; Jansson et al. 2007). This pattern is driven mainly by a change in the ratio of primary production to total respiration supported by an increase in allochthonous DOC. This suggests that allochthonous DOC plays an important regulatory role in lake metabolism through its influence on microbial metabolism (Jansson et al. 2000).

In this study, we investigated ten kettle holes in the north-eastern part of Germany representing a gradient in DOC concentrations and a broad diversity of kettle hole types. We aimed to assess the relative importance of allochthonous vs. autochthonous C for bacterial and biogeochemical processes in the water body. We hypothesized that allochthonous DOC is the major driver for bacterial activities in the water body of kettle holes and is more important than autochthonous C. In spring, early summer and autumn 2014, we measured various physico-chemical parameters such as nutrients (N and P), C quantity and quality (Liquid Chromatography with Organic Carbon Detection, absorbance), and bacterial protein production and community respiration in the water body of the kettle holes to evaluate the factors driving bacterial activities in kettle hole water bodies.

Methods

Study site

All kettle holes are located in the Quillow catchment in the temperate north-eastern part of Germany, approximately 80 km north-east of Berlin. A detailed description of the area can be found in Kleeberg et al. (2016). Ten kettle holes were selected (Table 1) based on a gradient in DOC concentrations. The 10 kettle holes in this study vary in size from 0.57 to \( 4.29 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{km}^2 \). All kettle holes exhibited macrophytes, but the dominant vegetation type differed. They were mostly vegetated by *Typha* spp., *Carex* spp., *Phragmites australis* or *Phalaris arundinacea* (Table 1). Despite variation in the dominant vegetation type, eight kettle holes were surrounded by arable land and two by grassland. All kettle holes were permanently flooded during the sampling campaign, except one of the smallest (identification number 3, see Table 1) which dried out in summer. Thus, the dataset was reduced to nine for early summer and autumn.

Sampling

The kettle holes were sampled three times during the growing season in 2014: in spring (February, 25th and 27th), early summer (June, 24th and 26th), and autumn (October, 28th and 30th). Temperature, pH and oxygen (\( \mathrm{O}_2 \)) concentrations were determined on site, using electronic probes (DurOx and SenTix for February and June as well as the Multi3410 probe for the October campaign; WTW, Weilheim, Germany). Surface water samples (~1 L) were taken

SP sampling point, L littoral, P pelagic, A area (ha), Z mean depth (m), Col water colour (absorbance at 430 nm) and HS Ratio humic substances ratio (ratio between the absorbances at 250/365 nm; Lakes: Ip Ipê, In Inácio, L2 Lake 02, Ho Horácio, Ri Rincão, Pa Passos, Sz Suzana, L10 Lake 10, Ve Veados, Ma Mangueira) total ‘Bacteria’ estimated by EUB338, ‘other Bacteria’ were considered equal to zero. Undetermined groups were computed as total DAPI counts (unspecific stain, total density) minus EUB338 counts or the sum (\( \alpha + \beta + \gamma \)-Proteobacteria + *Cytophaga Flavobacter*), whichever the highest. All probe counts were discounted from negative control (incompatible to any bacterial group). Bacterial biomass and proportion of bacterial morphotypes Samples were collected (as described in the Sampling section above) and fixed in 4% formaldehyde in polyethylene bottles in the field and stored in the dark and refrigerated until analysis. In the laboratory, samples were pre-filtered as described for the FISH protocol (above), and 2 ml of each sample were filtered (<5.0 kPa) in a Vacuum Manifold Filtration Tower (Millipore) with 1 ml of pre-filtered (0.2 \( \mu \)m) Mili-Q water to improve cell dispersion. Cells were concentrated onto 0.2 \( \mu \)m black polycarbonate membranes (GE). Approximately 1 ml of a 10% (weight:vol) acridine orange stain was added to the filters for 5 min. They were then washed with Mili-Q water and air dried. Filters were mounted in slides with mineral oil (Nujol) and were processed within 3 days because of quick faint of acridine. A total of 10 images were captured per filter and image processing was carried out on six of them. Image processing and capture were the same used for FISH, as described above, except for the microscope filter set, which was U-MWB2 460–490 nm for acridine. Once the images had been processed and binarised, they were submitted to CMEIAS software (Liu et al., 2001) for morphotype classification. This program consists of a series of plug-ins for Image Tool (1.27) and utilises multiple measures of each object, working hierarchically through statistical classification of 11 possible morphotypes (Coccus, Spiral, Curved Rod, U-Shaped Rod, Regular Rod, Unbranched Rod, Ellipsoid, Club, Prosthecate, Rudimentary Branched Rod and Branched Filament). Biovolume was calculated using specific equations for the different morphotypes by Massana et al. (1997) and converted to carbon (\( C = 0.09 \times \text{biovolume}^{0.9} \) in pgC; Norland, 1993). Bacterial production rate and duplication time of biomass Bacterial production was estimated using L[4.5-\(^3\)H] radio-labelled leucine and microcentrifugation (Simon & Azam, 1992; Kirchman, 2001). We added 11 \( \mu \)l of leucine solution (approximately 20 nmoles l\(^{-1}\), specific activity of 65 Ci mmol\(^{-1}\)) to 1,700 \( \mu \)l of sample in Eppendorf vials (2 ml). An initial vial was fixed at the beginning and 2–3 replicates for each sample were incubated for 30 min with lake water at room temperature (15–16\(^\circ\)C). Interruption of bacterial activity was achieved with the addition of 89 \( \mu \)l of concentrated trichloroacetic acid (TCA). The vials were then stored in the dark and cool until centrifugation. Samples were centrifuged three times (cell concentrate, +TCA 5% and +ethanol 80%) and left to dry for approximately 1 week at 4\(^\circ\)C. For beta-particles counting, 1 ml of scintillation liquid (Optiphase HiSafe III Wallac) was

SP sampling point, L littoral, P pelagic, A area (ha), Z mean depth (m), Col water colour (absorbance at 430 nm) and HS Ratio humic substances ratio (ratio between the absorbances at 250/365 nm; Lakes: Ip Ipê, In Inácio, L2 Lake 02, Ho Horácio, Ri Rincão, Pa Passos, Sz Suzana, L10 Lake 10, Ve Veados, Ma Mangueira) total ‘Bacteria’ estimated by EUB338, ‘other Bacteria’ were considered equal to zero. Undetermined groups were computed as total DAPI counts (unspecific stain, total density) minus EUB338 counts or the sum (\( \alpha + \beta + \gamma \)-Proteobacteria + *Cytophaga Flavobacter*), whichever the highest. All probe counts were discounted from negative control (incompatible to any bacterial group). Bacterial biomass and proportion of bacterial morphotypes Samples were collected (as described in the Sampling section above) and fixed in 4% formaldehyde in polyethylene bottles in the field and stored in the dark and refrigerated until analysis. In the laboratory, samples were pre-filtered as described for the FISH protocol (above), and 2 ml of each sample were filtered (<5.0 kPa) in a Vacuum Manifold Filtration Tower (Millipore) with 1 ml of pre-filtered (0.2 \( \mu \)m) Mili-Q water to improve cell dispersion. Cells were concentrated onto 0.2 \( \mu \)m black polycarbonate membranes (GE). Approximately 1 ml of a 10% (weight:vol) acridine orange stain was added to the filters for 5 min. They were then washed with Mili-Q water and air dried. Filters were mounted in slides with mineral oil (Nujol) and were processed within 3 days because of quick faint of acridine. A total of 10 images were captured per filter and image processing was carried out on six of them. Image processing and capture were the same used for FISH, as described above, except for the microscope filter set, which was U-MWB2 460–490 nm for acridine. Once the images had been processed and binarised, they were submitted to CMEIAS software (Liu et al., 2001) for morphotype classification. This program consists of a series of plug-ins for Image Tool (1.27) and utilises multiple measures of each object, working hierarchically through statistical classification of 11 possible morphotypes (Coccus, Spiral, Curved Rod, U-Shaped Rod, Regular Rod, Unbranched Rod, Ellipsoid, Club, Prosthecate, Rudimentary Branched Rod and Branched Filament). Biovolume was calculated using specific equations for the different morphotypes by Massana et al. (1997) and converted to carbon (\( C = 0.09 \times \text{biovolume}^{0.9} \) in pgC; Norland, 1993). Bacterial production rate and duplication time of biomass Bacterial production was estimated using L[4.5-\(^3\)H] radio-labelled leucine and microcentrifugation (Simon & Azam, 1992; Kirchman, 2001). We added 11 \( \mu \)l of leucine solution (approximately 20 nmoles l\(^{-1}\), specific activity of 65 Ci mmol\(^{-1}\)) to 1,700 \( \mu \)l of sample in Eppendorf vials (2 ml). An initial vial was fixed at the beginning and 2–3 replicates for each sample were incubated for 30 min with lake water at room temperature (15–16\(^\circ\)C). Interruption of bacterial activity was achieved with the addition of 89 \( \mu \)l of concentrated trichloroacetic acid (TCA). The vials were then stored in the dark and cool until centrifugation. Samples were centrifuged three times (cell concentrate, +TCA 5% and +ethanol 80%) and left to dry for approximately 1 week at 4\(^\circ\)C. For beta-particles counting, 1 ml of scintillation liquid (Optiphase HiSafe III Wallac) was

SP sampling point, L littoral, P pelagic, A area (ha), Z mean depth (m), Col water colour (absorbance at 430 nm) and HS Ratio humic substances ratio (ratio between the absorbances at 250/365 nm; Lakes: Ip Ipê, In Inácio, L2 Lake 02, Ho Horácio, Ri Rincão, Pa Passos, Sz Suzana, L10 Lake 10, Ve Veados, Ma Mangueira) total ‘Bacteria’ estimated by EUB338, ‘other Bacteria’ were considered equal to zero. Undetermined groups were computed as total DAPI counts (unspecific stain, total density) minus EUB338 counts or the sum (\( \alpha + \beta + \gamma \)-Proteobacteria + *Cytophaga Flavobacter*), whichever the highest. All probe counts were discounted from negative control (incompatible to any bacterial group). Bacterial biomass and proportion of bacterial morphotypes Samples were collected (as described in the Sampling section above) and fixed in 4% formaldehyde in polyethylene bottles in the field and stored in the dark and refrigerated until analysis. In the laboratory, samples were pre-filtered as described for the FISH protocol (above), and 2 ml of each sample were filtered (<5.0 kPa) in a Vacuum Manifold Filtration Tower (Millipore) with 1 ml of pre-filtered (0.2 \( \mu \)m) Mili-Q water to improve cell dispersion. Cells were concentrated onto 0.2 \( \mu \)m black polycarbonate membranes (GE). Approximately 1 ml of a 10% (weight:vol) acridine orange stain was added to the filters for 5 min. They were then washed with Mili-Q water and air dried. Filters were mounted in slides with mineral oil (Nujol) and were processed within 3 days because of quick faint of acridine. A total of 10 images were captured per filter and image processing was carried out on six of them. Image processing and capture were the same used for FISH, as described above, except for the microscope filter set, which was U-MWB2 460–490 nm for acridine. Once the images had been processed and binarised, they were submitted to CMEIAS software (Liu et al., 2001) for morphotype classification. This program consists of a series of plug-ins for Image Tool (1.27) and utilises multiple measures of each object, working hierarchically through statistical classification of 11 possible morphotypes (Coccus, Spiral, Curved Rod, U-Shaped Rod, Regular Rod, Unbranched Rod, Ellipsoid, Club, Prosthecate, Rudimentary Branched Rod and Branched Filament). Biovolume was calculated using specific equations for the different morphotypes by Massana et al. (1997) and converted to carbon (\( C = 0.09 \times \text{biovolume}^{0.9} \) in pgC; Norland, 1993). Bacterial production rate and duplication time of biomass Bacterial production was estimated using L[4.5-\(^3\)H] radio-labelled leucine and microcentrifugation (Simon & Azam, 1992; Kirchman, 2001). We added 11 \( \mu \)l of leucine solution (approximately 20 nmoles l\(^{-1}\), specific activity of 65 Ci mmol\(^{-1}\)) to 1,700 \( \mu \)l of sample in Eppendorf vials (2 ml). An initial vial was fixed at the beginning and 2–3 replicates for each sample were incubated for 30 min with lake water at room temperature (15–16\(^\circ\)C). Interruption of bacterial activity was achieved with the addition of 89 \( \mu \)l of concentrated trichloroacetic acid (TCA). The vials were then stored in the dark and cool until centrifugation. Samples were centrifuged three times (cell concentrate, +TCA 5% and +ethanol 80%) and left to dry for approximately 1 week at 4\(^\circ\)C. For beta-particles counting, 1 ml of scintillation liquid (Optiphase HiSafe III Wallac) was

name: Lake Ho

context:
Fig. 1. (a) Locations and configurations of (b) the Hsin-Hai II Constructed Wetland (HS2) and (c) Daniaopi Constructed Wetland (DN). 2.2. Environmental variables The wetland area of each treatment cell was estimated based on aerial photographs using the software package, Image Pro 4.0 (Media Cybernetics, Silver Spring, MD). Water variables were measured in the field using portable instruments, such as water temperature (WTW LF196, Weilheim, Germany), dissolved oxygen (WTW oxi 3302), conductivity (WTW Cond 330i), and pH (WTW pH340). Water samples were taken from the outflow of each cell, immediately placed on ice in a cooler, and brought back to the laboratory for analyses of BOD, COD, nitrate (NO₃⁻), nitrite (NO₂⁻), total nitrogen (TN), total phosphorus (TP), suspended solids (SS), and phytoplankton chlorophyll (Chl)-a concentration following standard methods of the APHA (Clesceri et al., 1998). 2.3. Biotic samples Aquatic macrophytes were surveyed using transects across treatment cells. Six transects were set up in each treatment cell, and all aquatic macrophytes on the transects were identified to species level. To determine the cover of aquatic macrophytes, we overlaid a grid of 2.5-m plots onto an enlarged aerial photo of each treatment cell, and the cover of each species was determined by estimating the fractions of vegetation in each grid and summing up all grids. When areal photos were not available, the cover of aquatic macrophytes was estimated on each transect in the field. We used a D-net sweep (Model 7412D, BioQuip, Rancho Dominguez, CA) to collect aquatic invertebrates modified from a procedure of the USEPA (2002). During each sweep, the D-net was placed on the wetland bottom and pushed horizontally into the vegetation, if available, for a distance of 1 m to cover an area of about 0.3 m², while bouncing the net into the sediment at least twice. The distance between locations of adjacent sweeps was at least 1 m. Two sweeps were combined into one sample, and three samples (six sweeps) were taken in each treatment cell. Materials collected in the net were poured into a 0.5-mm-mesh sieve. After elutriation and removal of large debris, samples retained on the sieve were preserved in 70% ethanol. Aquatic macroinverte-

brates were identified to the lowest possible taxonomic level and counted using a stereomicroscope in the laboratory. Nematodes and zooplankton taxa including cladoceran, copepods, and ostracods were excluded from further analyses. Major classes or orders were described for those specimens for which the species could not be determined. We used a cast net (2 m in radius with a 2-cm mesh size) and shrimp traps (15 cm in diameter) to collect fish from the constructed wetlands. The cast net was deployed three times in treatment cells where the method was applicable except in cells HS2-1, HS2-2, DN1, DN2, DN3-A, and DN3-D. Ten shrimp traps were also set up in each treatment cell. Caught fish were chilled on ice and transported back to the laboratory. A bird survey was conducted by three trained persons on three consecutive days, one for each constructed wetland. Observers patrolled the shores of treatment cells on foot during the first 3 h of daylight, and species and abundances in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands were determined with the aid of binoculars. The habitats where the birds were observed were also recorded. 2.4. Biotic metrics We assessed the biodiversity of constructed wetlands using two common measures of richness and the Shannon–Wiener index. Species richness (the number of species) was selected to represent the richness of birds, fishes, and aquatic macrophytes, whereas taxon richness (the number of taxa) was selected for aquatic macroinvertebrates in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands. The Shannon–Wiener index (\( H' \)) was calculated using the following equation: \[ H' = - \sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \ln p_i; \] where \( p_i \) is the proportion of individuals found of the ith species (taxon) or the percent coverage of the ith aquatic macrophyte species. 2.5. Statistical analysis Variables were first examined to see if they were normally distributed. DO, SS, BOD, COD, and NO\(_x^-\) (NO\(_2^-\) + NO\(_3^-\)) concentrations were log-transformed, while wetland areas and TN, TP, and phytoplankton Chl-\(a\) concentrations were square-root-transformed to improve the normality. Abundances of birds and fishes were square-root-transformed, and the density of aquatic macroinvertebrates was log(\(x + 1\))-transformed. An arcsine-square-root transformation was used for the percent cover of aquatic macrophytes (Zar, 1996). We used multiple regressions to develop the most appropriate statistical models to predict biodiversity measures of birds, fishes, and aquatic macroinvertebrates in the constructed wetlands. Species or taxon richness, abundance (density), and Shannon diversity (\(H'\)) of biotic communities were used as dependent variables, while wetland attributes, including wetland area, water quality, and biotic metrics, were used as independent variables in a stepwise regression model (Sokal and Rohlf, 1995). Variables with a significance level of <0.05 were included in the regression models. We did not examine the diversity of aquatic macrophytes because most of the aquatic macrophyte species were artificially introduced when constructing the wetlands. Moreover, we used multivariate techniques to examine relationships of the community structures of birds, fishes, and aquatic

The Shannon–Wiener index (\( H' \)) was calculated using the following equation: \[ H' = - \sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \ln p_i; \] where \( p_i \) is the proportion of individuals found of the ith species (taxon) or the percent coverage of the ith aquatic macrophyte species. 2.5. Statistical analysis Variables were first examined to see if they were normally distributed. DO, SS, BOD, COD, and NO\(_x^-\) (NO\(_2^-\) + NO\(_3^-\)) concentrations were log-transformed, while wetland areas and TN, TP, and phytoplankton Chl-\(a\) concentrations were square-root-transformed to improve the normality. Abundances of birds and fishes were square-root-transformed, and the density of aquatic macroinvertebrates was log(\(x + 1\))-transformed. An arcsine-square-root transformation was used for the percent cover of aquatic macrophytes (Zar, 1996). We used multiple regressions to develop the most appropriate statistical models to predict biodiversity measures of birds, fishes, and aquatic macroinvertebrates in the constructed wetlands. Species or taxon richness, abundance (density), and Shannon diversity (\(H'\)) of biotic communities were used as dependent variables, while wetland attributes, including wetland area, water quality, and biotic metrics, were used as independent variables in a stepwise regression model (Sokal and Rohlf, 1995). Variables with a significance level of <0.05 were included in the regression models. We did not examine the diversity of aquatic macrophytes because most of the aquatic macrophyte species were artificially introduced when constructing the wetlands. Moreover, we used multivariate techniques to examine relationships of the community structures of birds, fishes, and aquatic

brates were identified to the lowest possible taxonomic level and counted using a stereomicroscope in the laboratory. Nematodes and zooplankton taxa including cladoceran, copepods, and ostracods were excluded from further analyses. Major classes or orders were described for those specimens for which the species could not be determined. We used a cast net (2 m in radius with a 2-cm mesh size) and shrimp traps (15 cm in diameter) to collect fish from the constructed wetlands. The cast net was deployed three times in treatment cells where the method was applicable except in cells HS2-1, HS2-2, DN1, DN2, DN3-A, and DN3-D. Ten shrimp traps were also set up in each treatment cell. Caught fish were chilled on ice and transported back to the laboratory. A bird survey was conducted by three trained persons on three consecutive days, one for each constructed wetland. Observers patrolled the shores of treatment cells on foot during the first 3 h of daylight, and species and abundances in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands were determined with the aid of binoculars. brates were identified to the lowest possible taxonomic level and counted using a stereomicroscope in the laboratory. The cast net was deployed three times in treatment cells where the method was applicable except in cells HS2-1, HS2-2, DN1, DN2, DN3-A, and DN3-D. Ten shrimp traps were also set up in each treatment cell. Caught fish were chilled on ice and transported back to the laboratory. A bird survey was conducted by three trained persons on three consecutive days, one for each constructed wetland. Observers patrolled the shores of treatment cells on foot during the first 3 h of daylight, and species and abundances in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands were determined with the aid of binoculars. brates were identified to the lowest possible taxonomic level and counted using a stereomicroscope in the laboratory. Nematodes and zooplankton taxa including cladoceran, copepods, and ostracods were excluded from further analyses. Major classes or orders were described for those specimens for which the species could not be determined. We used a cast net (2 m in radius with a 2-cm mesh size) and shrimp traps (15 cm in diameter) to collect fish from the constructed wetlands. The cast net was deployed three times in treatment cells where the method was applicable except in cells HS2-1, HS2-2, DN1, DN2, DN3-A, and DN3-D. Ten shrimp traps were also set up in each treatment cell. Caught fish were chilled on ice and transported back to the laboratory. A bird survey was conducted by three trained persons on three consecutive days, one for each constructed wetland. Observers patrolled the shores of treatment cells on foot during the first 3 h of daylight, and species and abundances in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands were determined with the aid of binoculars. The habitats where the birds were observed were also recorded. 2.4. Biotic metrics We assessed the biodiversity of constructed wetlands using two common measures of richness and the Shannon–Wiener index. Species richness (the number of species) was selected to represent the richness of birds, fishes, and aquatic macrophytes, whereas taxon richness (the number of taxa) was selected for aquatic macroinvertebrates in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands. The Shannon–Wiener index (\( H' \)) was calculated using the following equation: \[ H' = - \sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \ln p_i; \] where \( p_i \) is the proportion of individuals found of the ith species (taxon) or the percent coverage of the ith aquatic macrophyte species. 2.5. Statistical analysis Variables were first examined to see if they were normally distributed. DO, SS, BOD, COD, and NO\(_x^-\) (NO\(_2^-\) + NO\(_3^-\)) concentrations were log-transformed, while wetland areas and TN, TP, and phytoplankton Chl-\(a\) concentrations were square-root-transformed to improve the normality. Abundances of birds and fishes were square-root-transformed, and the density of aquatic macroinvertebrates was log(\(x + 1\))-transformed. An arcsine-square-root transformation was used for the percent cover of aquatic macrophytes (Zar, 1996). We used multiple regressions to develop the most appropriate statistical models to predict biodiversity measures of birds, fishes, and aquatic macroinvertebrates in the constructed wetlands. Species or taxon richness, abundance (density), and Shannon diversity (\(H'\)) of biotic communities were used as dependent variables, while wetland attributes, including wetland area, water quality, and biotic metrics, were used as independent variables in a stepwise regression model (Sokal and Rohlf, 1995). Variables with a significance level of <0.05 were included in the regression models. We did not examine the diversity of aquatic macrophytes because most of the aquatic macrophyte species were artificially introduced when constructing the wetlands. Moreover, we used multivariate techniques to examine relationships of the community structures of birds, fishes, and aquatic

brates were identified to the lowest possible taxonomic level and counted using a stereomicroscope in the laboratory. Nematodes and zooplankton taxa including cladoceran, copepods, and ostracods were excluded from further analyses. Major classes or orders were described for those specimens for which the species could not be determined. We used a cast net (2 m in radius with a 2-cm mesh size) and shrimp traps (15 cm in diameter) to collect fish from the constructed wetlands. The cast net was deployed three times in treatment cells where the method was applicable except in cells HS2-1, HS2-2, DN1, DN2, DN3-A, and DN3-D. Ten shrimp traps were also set up in each treatment cell. Caught fish were chilled on ice and transported back to the laboratory. A bird survey was conducted by three trained persons on three consecutive days, one for each constructed wetland. Observers patrolled the shores of treatment cells on foot during the first 3 h of daylight, and species and abundances in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands were determined with the aid of binoculars. The habitats where the birds were observed were also recorded. 2.4. Biotic metrics We assessed the biodiversity of constructed wetlands using two common measures of richness and the Shannon–Wiener index. Species richness (the number of species) was selected to represent the richness of birds, fishes, and aquatic macrophytes, whereas taxon richness (the number of taxa) was selected for aquatic macroinvertebrates in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands. The Shannon–Wiener index (\( H' \)) was calculated using the following equation: \[ H' = - \sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \ln p_i; \] where \( p_i \) is the proportion of individuals found of the ith species (taxon) or the percent coverage of the ith aquatic macrophyte species. 2.5. Statistical analysis Variables were first examined to see if they were normally distributed. DO, SS, BOD, COD, and NO\(_x^-\) (NO\(_2^-\) + NO\(_3^-\)) concentrations were log-transformed, while wetland areas and TN, TP, and phytoplankton Chl-\(a\) concentrations were square-root-transformed to improve the normality. Abundances of birds and fishes were square-root-transformed, and the density of aquatic macroinvertebrates was log(\(x + 1\))-transformed. An arcsine-square-root transformation was used for the percent cover of aquatic macrophytes (Zar, 1996). We used multiple regressions to develop the most appropriate statistical models to predict biodiversity measures of birds, fishes, and aquatic macroinvertebrates in the constructed wetlands. Species or taxon richness, abundance (density), and Shannon diversity (\(H'\)) of biotic communities were used as dependent variables, while wetland attributes, including wetland area, water quality, and biotic metrics, were used as independent variables in a stepwise regression model (Sokal and Rohlf, 1995). Variables with a significance level of <0.05 were included in the regression models. We did not examine the diversity of aquatic macrophytes because most of the aquatic macrophyte species were artificially introduced when constructing the wetlands. Moreover, we used multivariate techniques to examine relationships of the community structures of birds, fishes, and aquatic

Stilt (Himantopus himantopus), Greater Painted Snipe (Rostratula benghalensis), Little Egret (Egretta garzetta), Black-crowned Night Heron (Nycticorax nycticorax), Yellow-headed Fantail Warbler (Cisticola exilis), Rufous Fantail Warbler (Cisticola juncidis), and White-breasted Waterhen (Amaurornis phoenicurus) were more abundant in treatment cells with high water temperature, fish richness and abundance, and macrophyte richness, whereas Dusky Thrush (Turdus naumanni), Teal (Anas crecca), and Yellow Wagtail (Motacilla flava) were negatively related to water temperature (Fig. 4). Japanese White-eye (Zosterops japonica), Red Turtle Dove (Streptopelia tranquebarica), Oriental Turtle Dove (S. orientalis), and Barn Swallow (Hirundo rustica) were mainly distributed in cells with large areas and high concentrations of phytoplankton Chl-\( a \) and SS and pH values (Fig. 4). Cattle Egret (Bubulcus ibis), Common Moorhen (Gallinula chloropus), Yellow-bellied Prinia (Prinia flaviventris), Tawny Prinia (P. subflava), and Spotted-necked Dove (Streptopelia chinensis) were positively correlated with the density of aquatic macroinvertebrates (Fig. 4). name: constructed wetland

context:
Fig. 1. Rittgarten kettle hole, Uckermark, Northeastern Germany (photo by G. Kazanjian).

lenses of weathered till (Merz and Steidl, 2015). Residence time within the kettle hole basins, assessed via isotope data, is usually in the order of a few years (Nitzsche et al., 2017) which is very long compared to the kinetics of internal processes.

Model forcing functions and calibration were mainly based on a dataset available at ZALF Open Research Data (dataset Kleeberg et al., 2018). Briefly, water samples were collected at 0.3 m depth from July 4th 2013 to July 22th 2014 with biweekly to monthly frequency (except in January 2014). Total phosphorus (TP), phosphate (PO₄), total nitrogen (TN), ammonium (NH₄), nitrate (NO₃), total organic carbon (TOC), dissolved organic carbon (DOC) and chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) were determined using standard methods as described in Kleeberg et al. (2016a). Total inorganic carbon and dissolved inorganic carbon (TIC and DIC) were determined using a TOC and TN analyzer (multi N/C® 3100, Jena Analytics, Germany) with Non-Dispersive Infrared detector (NDIR) according to DIN EN 1484-H3 (DEV, 2003). Total carbon (TC) was then calculated as the sum of TIC and TOC.

Water temperature, dissolved oxygen (DO) and pH were measured in situ at 0.3 m water depth (WTW CellOx® 325 sensor coupled to a ProfiLine Oxi 3205 and SenTix® 81 pH electrode coupled to a ProfiLine pH 3110 portable meter, respectively). Because water level declined markedly during the study period (Kleeberg et al., 2016a), the distance between the sampling depth (0.3 m) and the sediment decreased accordingly. The water volume dynamics of Rittgarten (Fig. 1, Supplementary Material) were based on water level data measured both manually (with weekly to monthly frequency) but also automatically every 30 min by a CS451 pressure transducer (Campbell Scientific, USA9). This information was combined with site-specific bathymetric and topographic data (Kleeberg et al., 2016b). Water temperature was measured every 30 min by a CS451 pressure transducer (Campbell Scientific, USA9). Daily mean photosynthetic active radiation (PAR) was approximated by a sinusoidal function, based on data recorded every 30 min at the CarboZALF experimental area, located ≈ 6 km from Rittgarten. The free-floating macrophyte and submerged macrophyte biomass determined in July 2013 (77 ± 28 g dry weight m⁻² and 125 ± 42 g dry weight m⁻², respectively; Kleeberg et al., 2016a), was assumed to represent the growing season biomass maxima. Similar to Kazanjian et al. (2018) we calculated the biomass seasonal variation by fitting a polynomial curve and considering that biomass was negligible prior to May and after September.

2.2. Model structure and forcing functions

The model targets the reproduction of seasonal patterns of a number of water quality variables and the primary producers, i.e., phytoplankton and free-floating macrophytes in Rittgarten. Mathematical description of the model is presented in Table 1 and model parameters are defined in Table 2. The Rittgarten model partly builds upon the Lake Washington and Albufera de Valencia eutrophication models (Arhonditsis and Brett 2005a, 2005b; Onandia et al., 2015). Postulating spatial homogeneity in the kettle hole, we developed a single compartment model that depicts four elemental cycles: carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus and oxygen. The model considers the interplay among the following state variables: phytoplankton, free-floating macrophytes, DIC, DOC, POC, NH₄, NO₃, organic N (ON), PO₄, organic P (OP) and DO. The external forcing functions of the model comprise submerged macrophytes biomass, daily mean water temperature, daily mean solar PAR, day length, water level, water pH and TIC (Fig. 1, Supplementary Material). There was no external nutrient input via surface run-off associated to snow melt or heavy rain events (Kazanjian et al., 2018; Thomas Kalettka, personal communication). Further, Rittgarten is surrounded by reed belt and presents a fairly flat topography. Therefore, we assumed that potential pulses of matter input from the catchment did not significantly affect biogeochemical processes in the water body at the time scale of our study and no external nutrient inputs were considered in our modeling exercise.

2.3. Model equations

2.3.1. Phytoplankton

Given the lack of specific information on the composition of the phytoplankton community in Rittgarten, the model considers a single generic phytoplankton group. The phytoplankton equation considers biomass production and losses due to mortality and settling. Phytoplankton production is modeled using a multiplicative function that accounts for the influence of temperature, light, and nutrient availability (Cerco and Cole, 1995). Phytoplankton N and P uptake depends on both intracellular and extracellular concentrations and is constrained by upper and lower internal storage capacity levels. Therefore, N and P dynamics within the phytoplankton cells consider luxury uptake (Hamilton and Schladow, 1997), whereas a Monod-type function was used to depict C uptake solely as a function of extracellular
name: Rittgarten kettle hole

context:
Fig. 1. Rittgarten kettle hole, Uckermark, Northeastern Germany (photo by G. Kazanjian).

lenses of weathered till (Merz and Steidl, 2015). Residence time within the kettle hole basins, assessed via isotope data, is usually in the order of a few years (Nitzsche et al., 2017) which is very long compared to the kinetics of internal processes.

Model forcing functions and calibration were mainly based on a dataset available at ZALF Open Research Data (dataset Kleeberg et al., 2018). Briefly, water samples were collected at 0.3 m depth from July 4th 2013 to July 22th 2014 with biweekly to monthly frequency (except in January 2014). Total phosphorus (TP), phosphate (PO₄), total nitrogen (TN), ammonium (NH₄), nitrate (NO₃), total organic carbon (TOC), dissolved organic carbon (DOC) and chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) were determined using standard methods as described in Kleeberg et al. (2016a). Total inorganic carbon and dissolved inorganic carbon (TIC and DIC) were determined using a TOC and TN analyzer (multi N/C® 3100, Jena Analytics, Germany) with Non-Dispersive Infrared detector (NDIR) according to DIN EN 1484-H3 (DEV, 2003). Total carbon (TC) was then calculated as the sum of TIC and TOC.

Water temperature, dissolved oxygen (DO) and pH were measured in situ at 0.3 m water depth (WTW CellOx® 325 sensor coupled to a ProfiLine Oxi 3205 and SenTix® 81 pH electrode coupled to a ProfiLine pH 3110 portable meter, respectively). Because water level declined markedly during the study period (Kleeberg et al., 2016a), the distance between the sampling depth (0.3 m) and the sediment decreased accordingly. The water volume dynamics of Rittgarten (Fig. 1, Supplementary Material) were based on water level data measured both manually (with weekly to monthly frequency) but also automatically every 30 min by a CS451 pressure transducer (Campbell Scientific, USA9). This information was combined with site-specific bathymetric and topographic data (Kleeberg et al., 2016b). Water temperature was measured every 30 min by a CS451 pressure transducer (Campbell Scientific, USA9). Daily mean photosynthetic active radiation (PAR) was approximated by a sinusoidal function, based on data recorded every 30 min at the CarboZALF experimental area, located ≈ 6 km from Rittgarten. The free-floating macrophyte and submerged macrophyte biomass determined in July 2013 (77 ± 28 g dry weight m⁻² and 125 ± 42 g dry weight m⁻², respectively; Kleeberg et al., 2016a), was assumed to represent the growing season biomass maxima. Similar to Kazanjian et al. (2018) we calculated the biomass seasonal variation by fitting a polynomial curve and considering that biomass was negligible prior to May and after September.

2.2. Model structure and forcing functions

The model targets the reproduction of seasonal patterns of a number of water quality variables and the primary producers, i.e., phytoplankton and free-floating macrophytes in Rittgarten. Mathematical description of the model is presented in Table 1 and model parameters are defined in Table 2. The Rittgarten model partly builds upon the Lake Washington and Albufera de Valencia eutrophication models (Arhonditsis and Brett 2005a, 2005b; Onandia et al., 2015). Postulating spatial homogeneity in the kettle hole, we developed a single compartment model that depicts four elemental cycles: carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus and oxygen. The model considers the interplay among the following state variables: phytoplankton, free-floating macrophytes, DIC, DOC, POC, NH₄, NO₃, organic N (ON), PO₄, organic P (OP) and DO. The external forcing functions of the model comprise submerged macrophytes biomass, daily mean water temperature, daily mean solar PAR, day length, water level, water pH and TIC (Fig. 1, Supplementary Material). There was no external nutrient input via surface run-off associated to snow melt or heavy rain events (Kazanjian et al., 2018; Thomas Kalettka, personal communication). Further, Rittgarten is surrounded by reed belt and presents a fairly flat topography. Therefore, we assumed that potential pulses of matter input from the catchment did not significantly affect biogeochemical processes in the water body at the time scale of our study and no external nutrient inputs were considered in our modeling exercise.

Phytoplankton N and P uptake depends on both intracellular and extracellular concentrations and is constrained by upper and lower internal storage capacity levels. Therefore, N and P dynamics within the phytoplankton cells consider luxury uptake (Hamilton and Schladow, 1997), whereas a Monod-type function was used to depict C uptake solely as a function of extracellular

2.3.1. Phytoplankton

name: kettle hole

context:
ratio), computed in the same way as for BPS; (ii) aquatic macrophyte coverage similarity all computed from each zone separately and (iii) area similarity between lakes, measured as {1 − (standardised Manhattan distance)} assumed to be identical for each zone. Standardisation for Euclidean distance was based on the total and for Manhattan distance by range in order to obtain similarity indexes between 0 and 1.

For environmental variables, DOC:PO$_4^{3-}$ and DOC:NO$_3^-$ are considered as measures of the proportion of organic to inorganic nutrients in the system, which is assumed to decrease along trophic gradients (Cotner & Biddanda, 2002). We also included TOC:TN; TN:TP, referring to the classical Redfield ratio; DOC:TOC, the ratio of dissolved to total carbon, which is assumed to positively favour bacterioplankton (Cotner & Biddanda, 2002), and the bacterial to phytoplankton carbon biomass ratio, which is often higher in oligotrophic systems (del Giorgio et al., 1997; Cotner & Biddanda, 2002).

Statistical tests

All bacterioplankton variables (BBiov, BBiom, BP and DT) were tested (after log (x + 1) transformation) for association with lake area (log transformed) for both the littoral and the pelagic zone in order to avoid pseudoreplication (\( n = 10 \)). In order to assess whether the difference between lake zones could be associated with lake area, littoral minus pelagic differences (also after log (x + 1) transformation) of all limnological and bacterioplankton variables were tested against lake area (log transformed). These associations were tested through linear regression in PAST v.1.80 (Hammer et al., 2001).

Differences between littoral and pelagic zones of all lakes and also groups of lakes arranged according to area were tested using ANOSIM. For this analysis we included, besides the variables listed above, bacterial phylotypes and morphotypes. For these we used the Morisita–Horn similarity index (\( C_\lambda = 2 \sum n_{1i} n_{2i} / (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2) N_1 N_2 \) with \( \lambda_j = \sum n_{ji}^2 / N_j^2 \); \( n_{ji} \) is the number of individuals of species \( i \) in the sample \( j \) and \( N_j \) is the number of individuals in sample \( j \)), the same used for the construction of the similarity matrices, as it is very robust to sample size and gives more weight to abundance than presence/absence (Wolda, 1981). For others, we employed the Euclidean distance (often used for environmental variables).

Computations were performed using PAST v.1.80 (Hammer et al., 2001). For the similarity matrix approach we first calculated the Pearson’s correlation to test for relationships between the similarity matrices of the bacterial variables.

We elucidated the effects of environmental variables on the four bacterial variables testing the explanatory power of environmental similarity, lake area similarity and the three types of macrophyte coverage similarity on bacterial production, biomass duplication time, bacterial assembly composition and bacterial morphotype proportion. We first employed all possible pairwise comparisons and then separated the similarity between only littoral and between only pelagic zones. For this similarity matrices association we used linear regression between matrices. We aligned the matrices in order to match each similarity index between two sampling points to another index between the same points and calculated the linear regression parameters. Computations were performed in FitoPacShell (v. 1.6.4.25)—Morisita–Horn similarity matrices, Multiv (v.2.3.10)—Euclidean distance matrices, R (v. 2.6.0) and Origin (v. 6.0) Linear Regressions and graphic presentation, respectively.

Results

Overall, in spite of differences in morphometric variables and human activities intensity in the catchment where the lakes are located, the lakes are similar in nutrients concentration and other physical and chemical variables (Tables 1, 2 and Figs. 1, 2, 3). ANOSIM revealed that the littoral and pelagic zones of the lakes differ only for the three types of macrophyte coverage (higher in littoral zones); the smallest lakes differed from the other lakes for TN, Chl \( a \) and the Bbiom:Pbiom ratio (higher in the smallest lakes) while the largest lakes differed from the others for Alk, Col, Turb, HS, DOC:TOC, DT and emergent, floating and submerged macrophyte coverage (except for Col, all the other variables were higher in the largest lakes) (Table 3). The regression of all bacterioplankton variables against lake area revealed that only littoral bacterioplankton production was related to lake area, decreasing along a lake area gradient (\( \log(\text{BP} + 1) = -0.04(\log \text{area}) + 0.03, R^2 = 0.77 \)).

EMCS, FMCS and SMCS are Emergent, Floating and Submersed macrophyte coverage similarity, respectively & del Giorgio, 2003), explaining why this technique has only seldom been employed to assess the physiological status of the bacterial assembly (Bouvier & del Giorgio, 2003). Moreover, the large number of bacteria associated with the ‘undetermined group’ class may likely reflect low metabolic activity (few ribosomes copies) rather than provide a snapshot of major bacterial groups diversity; thus, we decided to use FISH data for comparisons with caution when discerning diversity patterns. To improve assessment of diversity patterns future works ought to include the universal bacterial domain cocktail of probes EUBMIX, which targets more than 90% of the bacteria, and/or use CARD-FISH, which relies less on the ribosomic pool inside the cells. We found that similarities in the bacterioplankton phylotypes detection, production, duplication time and morphotypes were related to similarities in environmental physico-chemical variables and, for some variables, also to lake area. The importance of similarities in environmental variables (ES) and similarities in lake area (LAS) in littoral zones was higher, suggesting that the littoral data are mainly responsible for the overall dependence of bacterial variables on ES and LAS. We found increasing HS, TN, DOC:PO\(_4^{3-}\), DOC:NO\(_3^-\), TN:TP, BP, and DT values and decreasing TSS and floating emergent macrophyte coverage along the lake area gradient. In order to test the effect of increasing lake area on increasing differences between littoral and pelagic zones of the lakes, we subtracted, for all variables sampled, the values of littoral minus pelagic zone of each lake and regressed the differences with lake area. This approach revealed that these differences decreased for chlorophyll \( a \) and increased for emergent macrophyte coverage with increasing lake area. However, due to lack of catchment information for some of the lakes (Table 2), it is not possible to test whether the difference is actually a result of change in lake area or of differences in the loading of nutrients to the lakes. In addition, BPS, DTS, BAS and BMS correlated equally well with similarities of macrophyte coverage

ABSTRACT
Constructed wetlands are often built for wastewater treatment to mitigate the adverse effects of organic pollution in streams and rivers caused by inputs of municipal wastewater. However, there has been little analysis of biodiversity and related factors influencing the ecosystem functioning of constructed wetlands. The purpose of this study was to evaluate the biodiversity of two free-water-surface integrated constructed wetlands in subtropical Taiwan by analyzing the water quality, habitat characteristics, and biotic communities of algae, macrophytes, birds, fish, and aquatic macroinvertebrates in the treatment cells. Our results indicated that the two integrated constructed wetlands (Hsin-Hai II and Daniaopi Constructed Wetlands) achieved good performance in reducing the concentrations of total nitrogen (TN) and total phosphorus (TP), and loadings of biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) and chemical oxygen demand (COD) from municipal sewage. In total, 58 bird species, 7 fish species, and 34 aquatic macroinvertebrate taxa were recorded in the two wetlands. The results of stepwise multiple regressions showed that the richness, abundance, and diversity of birds increased with wetland area. Fish richness and abundance respectively increased with wetland area and dissolved oxygen, while the diversity decreased with increases in TP concentrations. The richness and density of aquatic macroinvertebrates increased with the cover of aquatic macrophytes, while the diversity increased with wetland area. Ordination analyses indicated that variations in the community structures of birds, fishes, and aquatic macroinvertebrates were respectively best explained by water temperature, wetland area, and species richness of fish. Our results suggest that wetland area, cover of aquatic macrophytes, and water quality were the most important factors governing the diversity in the constructed wetlands, and that the factors influencing community structures varied among different taxonomic groups. In addition to improving water quality, this study implied that the biodiversity of constructed wetlands for wastewater treatment can be enhanced through proper design and management.

1. Introduction

Due to increasing population densities and impacts from human activities, pollution remains an essential issue for water quality protection and conservation of biodiversity in urban areas. There is a wide range of treatment technologies that can be used to improve the quality of wastewater to meet effluent standards. Many regions, however, do not have purification plants or lack sufficient capacity to treat all effluents due to the high costs of treatment processes (Kivaisi, 2002). The low coverage of sewage systems often results in municipal sewage and industrial wastewater being directly discharged into aquatic environments with no treatment (Jing et al., 2008).

Wetlands are well known as one of the most productive ecosystems in the world (Mitsch and Gosselink, 2007). Countless species of birds, mammals, reptiles, amphibians, fishes, and invertebrates depend on the water and vegetation in wetland habitats (Zedler and Kercher, 2005; Mitsch and Gosselink, 2007; Kadlec and Wallace, 2008). Wetlands also directly and indirectly support people by providing ecosystem services such as flood abatement, food, a clean water supply, esthetic beauty, educational and recreational

brates were identified to the lowest possible taxonomic level and counted using a stereomicroscope in the laboratory. Nematodes and zooplankton taxa including cladoceran, copepods, and ostracods were excluded from further analyses. Major classes or orders were described for those specimens for which the species could not be determined. We used a cast net (2 m in radius with a 2-cm mesh size) and shrimp traps (15 cm in diameter) to collect fish from the constructed wetlands. The cast net was deployed three times in treatment cells where the method was applicable except in cells HS2-1, HS2-2, DN1, DN2, DN3-A, and DN3-D. Ten shrimp traps were also set up in each treatment cell. Caught fish were chilled on ice and transported back to the laboratory. A bird survey was conducted by three trained persons on three consecutive days, one for each constructed wetland. Observers patrolled the shores of treatment cells on foot during the first 3 h of daylight, and species and abundances in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands were determined with the aid of binoculars. The habitats where the birds were observed were also recorded. 2.4. Biotic metrics We assessed the biodiversity of constructed wetlands using two common measures of richness and the Shannon–Wiener index. Species richness (the number of species) was selected to represent the richness of birds, fishes, and aquatic macrophytes, whereas taxon richness (the number of taxa) was selected for aquatic macroinvertebrates in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands. The Shannon–Wiener index (\( H' \)) was calculated using the following equation: \[ H' = - \sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \ln p_i; \] where \( p_i \) is the proportion of individuals found of the ith species (taxon) or the percent coverage of the ith aquatic macrophyte species. 2.5. Statistical analysis Variables were first examined to see if they were normally distributed. DO, SS, BOD, COD, and NO\(_x^-\) (NO\(_2^-\) + NO\(_3^-\)) concentrations were log-transformed, while wetland areas and TN, TP, and phytoplankton Chl-\(a\) concentrations were square-root-transformed to improve the normality. Abundances of birds and fishes were square-root-transformed, and the density of aquatic macroinvertebrates was log(\(x + 1\))-transformed. An arcsine-square-root transformation was used for the percent cover of aquatic macrophytes (Zar, 1996). We used multiple regressions to develop the most appropriate statistical models to predict biodiversity measures of birds, fishes, and aquatic macroinvertebrates in the constructed wetlands. Species or taxon richness, abundance (density), and Shannon diversity (\(H'\)) of biotic communities were used as dependent variables, while wetland attributes, including wetland area, water quality, and biotic metrics, were used as independent variables in a stepwise regression model (Sokal and Rohlf, 1995). Variables with a significance level of <0.05 were included in the regression models. We did not examine the diversity of aquatic macrophytes because most of the aquatic macrophyte species were artificially introduced when constructing the wetlands. Moreover, we used multivariate techniques to examine relationships of the community structures of birds, fishes, and aquatic

brates were identified to the lowest possible taxonomic level and counted using a stereomicroscope in the laboratory. Nematodes and zooplankton taxa including cladoceran, copepods, and ostracods were excluded from further analyses. Major classes or orders were described for those specimens for which the species could not be determined. We used a cast net (2 m in radius with a 2-cm mesh size) and shrimp traps (15 cm in diameter) to collect fish from the constructed wetlands. The cast net was deployed three times in treatment cells where the method was applicable except in cells HS2-1, HS2-2, DN1, DN2, DN3-A, and DN3-D. Ten shrimp traps were also set up in each treatment cell. Caught fish were chilled on ice and transported back to the laboratory. A bird survey was conducted by three trained persons on three consecutive days, one for each constructed wetland. Observers patrolled the shores of treatment cells on foot during the first 3 h of daylight, and species and abundances in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands were determined with the aid of binoculars. The habitats where the birds were observed were also recorded. 2.4. Biotic metrics We assessed the biodiversity of constructed wetlands using two common measures of richness and the Shannon–Wiener index. Species richness (the number of species) was selected to represent the richness of birds, fishes, and aquatic macrophytes, whereas taxon richness (the number of taxa) was selected for aquatic macroinvertebrates in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands. The Shannon–Wiener index (\( H' \)) was calculated using the following equation: \[ H' = - \sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \ln p_i; \] where \( p_i \) is the proportion of individuals found of the ith species (taxon) or the percent coverage of the ith aquatic macrophyte species. 2.5. Statistical analysis Variables were first examined to see if they were normally distributed. DO, SS, BOD, COD, and NO\(_x^-\) (NO\(_2^-\) + NO\(_3^-\)) concentrations were log-transformed, while wetland areas and TN, TP, and phytoplankton Chl-\(a\) concentrations were square-root-transformed to improve the normality. Abundances of birds and fishes were square-root-transformed, and the density of aquatic macroinvertebrates was log(\(x + 1\))-transformed. An arcsine-square-root transformation was used for the percent cover of aquatic macrophytes (Zar, 1996). We used multiple regressions to develop the most appropriate statistical models to predict biodiversity measures of birds, fishes, and aquatic macroinvertebrates in the constructed wetlands. Species or taxon richness, abundance (density), and Shannon diversity (\(H'\)) of biotic communities were used as dependent variables, while wetland attributes, including wetland area, water quality, and biotic metrics, were used as independent variables in a stepwise regression model (Sokal and Rohlf, 1995). Variables with a significance level of <0.05 were included in the regression models. We did not examine the diversity of aquatic macrophytes because most of the aquatic macrophyte species were artificially introduced when constructing the wetlands. Moreover, we used multivariate techniques to examine relationships of the community structures of birds, fishes, and aquatic

Species richness (the number of species) was selected to represent the richness of birds, fishes, and aquatic macrophytes, whereas taxon richness (the number of taxa) was selected for aquatic macroinvertebrates in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands. The Shannon–Wiener index (\( H' \)) was calculated using the following equation: \[ H' = - \sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \ln p_i; \] where \( p_i \) is the proportion of individuals found of the ith species (taxon) or the percent coverage of the ith aquatic macrophyte species. 2.5. Statistical analysis Variables were first examined to see if they were normally distributed. DO, SS, BOD, COD, and NO\(_x^-\) (NO\(_2^-\) + NO\(_3^-\)) concentrations were log-transformed, while wetland areas and TN, TP, and phytoplankton Chl-\(a\) concentrations were square-root-transformed to improve the normality. Abundances of birds and fishes were square-root-transformed, and the density of aquatic macroinvertebrates was log(\(x + 1\))-transformed. An arcsine-square-root transformation was used for the percent cover of aquatic macrophytes (Zar, 1996). We used multiple regressions to develop the most appropriate statistical models to predict biodiversity measures of birds, fishes, and aquatic macroinvertebrates in the constructed wetlands. Species or taxon richness, abundance (density), and Shannon diversity (\(H'\)) of biotic communities were used as dependent variables, while wetland attributes, including wetland area, water quality, and biotic metrics, were used as independent variables in a stepwise regression model (Sokal and Rohlf, 1995). Variables with a significance level of <0.05 were included in the regression models. We did not examine the diversity of aquatic macrophytes because most of the aquatic macrophyte species were artificially introduced when constructing the wetlands. Moreover, we used multivariate techniques to examine relationships of the community structures of birds, fishes, and aquatic

brates were identified to the lowest possible taxonomic level and counted using a stereomicroscope in the laboratory. brates were identified to the lowest possible taxonomic level and counted using a stereomicroscope in the laboratory. brates were identified to the lowest possible taxonomic level and counted using a stereomicroscope in the laboratory. Nematodes and zooplankton taxa including cladoceran, copepods, and ostracods were excluded from further analyses. Major classes or orders were described for those specimens for which the species could not be determined. We used a cast net (2 m in radius with a 2-cm mesh size) and shrimp traps (15 cm in diameter) to collect fish from the constructed wetlands. The cast net was deployed three times in treatment cells where the method was applicable except in cells HS2-1, HS2-2, DN1, DN2, DN3-A, and DN3-D. Ten shrimp traps were also set up in each treatment cell. Caught fish were chilled on ice and transported back to the laboratory. A bird survey was conducted by three trained persons on three consecutive days, one for each constructed wetland. Observers patrolled the shores of treatment cells on foot during the first 3 h of daylight, and species and abundances in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands were determined with the aid of binoculars. The habitats where the birds were observed were also recorded. 2.4. Biotic metrics We assessed the biodiversity of constructed wetlands using two common measures of richness and the Shannon–Wiener index. Species richness (the number of species) was selected to represent the richness of birds, fishes, and aquatic macrophytes, whereas taxon richness (the number of taxa) was selected for aquatic macroinvertebrates in each treatment cell of the two constructed wetlands. The Shannon–Wiener index (\( H' \)) was calculated using the following equation: \[ H' = - \sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \ln p_i; \] where \( p_i \) is the proportion of individuals found of the ith species (taxon) or the percent coverage of the ith aquatic macrophyte species. 2.5. Statistical analysis Variables were first examined to see if they were normally distributed. DO, SS, BOD, COD, and NO\(_x^-\) (NO\(_2^-\) + NO\(_3^-\)) concentrations were log-transformed, while wetland areas and TN, TP, and phytoplankton Chl-\(a\) concentrations were square-root-transformed to improve the normality. Abundances of birds and fishes were square-root-transformed, and the density of aquatic macroinvertebrates was log(\(x + 1\))-transformed. An arcsine-square-root transformation was used for the percent cover of aquatic macrophytes (Zar, 1996). We used multiple regressions to develop the most appropriate statistical models to predict biodiversity measures of birds, fishes, and aquatic macroinvertebrates in the constructed wetlands. Species or taxon richness, abundance (density), and Shannon diversity (\(H'\)) of biotic communities were used as dependent variables, while wetland attributes, including wetland area, water quality, and biotic metrics, were used as independent variables in a stepwise regression model (Sokal and Rohlf, 1995). Variables with a significance level of <0.05 were included in the regression models. We did not examine the diversity of aquatic macrophytes because most of the aquatic macrophyte species were artificially introduced when constructing the wetlands. Moreover, we used multivariate techniques to examine relationships of the community structures of birds, fishes, and aquatic
